speaker: interviewee
text: They took me to the station, the railroad station. I had to go to Paris and from there, take a train to Le Havre. It had not-- I was so busy with all these things that are happening to me that it never really sank in that I was going totally by myself. I had never been by myself, never. I came from a very loving house. We had a [? family. ?] I was [INAUDIBLE] sent me God knows where. I started to cry. And I said, you know, I changed my mind. I don't want to go. I'll wait for you. I'll wait. No, you have to go. You have to [? be the start. ?] And without you, we won't get anything. You have to make sure that we get the affidavits for, for the rest of us, and so on and so forth. You have to go. And I started to cry. And they pushed me on the train. And I'll never forget. I was hanging out the door, crying bitterly while I waved goodbye. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a woman sitting in, in the train. And she wanted to know why I was crying. And she says, 'Where are you going?' And I said, 'I'm going to the United States.' She said, you shouldn't be crying. You don't know how many people are envying you. You should be, you know? But it was-- I mean, I cried most of the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had given me lunch. So I didn't have to spend--you could only take out much mon-- as-- you couldn't take out any money, only as much that would mean, like, $10 here. So I had that. And I wasn't going to spend any of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Paris. Somebody from the boat picked us up. There were a few of us, apparently. They picked them up from all over and took us to Le Havre. And we went into this little boat. I looked at this little ship. It was, was one of those-- it was called the SS Roosevelt, but it was Teddy Roosevelt, not Franklin Roosevelt. It was named after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a small boat-- it looked awful small to me-- that was going to take us across the country. They had, in a hurry, made up a lot of little boats. Because it, it wasn't a luxury boat. It only had one class. Everybody had the same class. Because a lot of people want to go, so they got those things together. It was supposed to be five to six days. It turned out to be 11 days. We ran into a terrible, terrible storm. And the boat was pushed all over the place. And a lot of people got deathly sick. To my surprise, I didn't. I got hungry. I ate myself across. I don't know, the sea air or something, I didn't get sick. I ate myself across there. I was the only one that always showed up for dinner, for extra food, anything. And I-- we came to New York on a, on a, on a, a November day. It was raining. And I couldn't see the Statue of Liberty, which I was looking forward to. Because it was raining so much, you couldn't see anything. It was foggy and rainy. And when the, the boat docked, we had to go through customs. And and, you know, a lot of us went upstairs and looked down. And there were a lot of people standing downstairs looking for their relatives. And somebody called my name. And there were my, my great uncle and my great aunt standing there. She was very well dressed with a little hat and a veil and an umbrella. And he had just a cap on, one of those, you know, ordinary caps and some baggy pants. And he didn't have an umbrella, but she did. And they, they called my name. And then when I got down, finally introduced myself. And to my so relief, they spoke German. I mean, they called themselves Hungarian. A lot of Austrians in those days called themselves Hungarian. I didn't mention that. Because it was the Hungarian-- but if they were from that part only, they called themselves Hungarian. But they spoke German. In those days, they-- most of the people there spoke three languages, spoke German, Czech, and, and, and Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they spoke Hungarian. And they spoke German and English, of course. But their English was very bad. Even I could hear that they had a thick accent. They were here over 30 years. They had thick accents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me to Brooklyn. And I got to see the-- my first house in, in the United States. She had a very nice apartment with one bedroom-- very nice, lovely apartment with an elevator and hot running water. I'd never seen that before, you know, and heat that she didn't have to-- I didn't mention that. We had, had to get up in the morning and make heat in the living room with coal. That, I passed that up-- wasn't important, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, it was hot running water and a bath tub. And, and it was just-- to me, it was [INAUDIBLE]. It was wonderful. And I was told, this is the bedroom. There were twin beds there. Twin, there were twin beds there. And she says, you sleep in other. And your uncle sleeps in the other. I thought it was a strange room. And I said, where do you sleep? She slept in the living room on a pull-out couch.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't apparently had this arrangement. It had nothing to do with me. That has been going on whether I slept there or not. He slept in the bedroom alone. And she slept in the living room. And sometimes, her sons would come out and sleep on the other two couches. That's all they had was a couch. And I'd never seen American-- we had this great, big furniture, you know, with the-- with dishes and all these great big things and great big th-- they had just the couch and a little table. And to me, this was strange. The room was empty to me. Well anyway-- and that's when I found out what my uncle did for a living. He did have a job. He ran a pool hall. I had never seen a pool hall in my life. I didn't even know what billiards was. And he took me there. And he was showed me off to his pals there. Because apparently, he did it to show off. He did something for someone. He made-- it made him good in the eyes of, of people. Because he brought somebody from the claws of Hitler. Now my, my aunt, on the other hand, she had been against it. Apparently, my grandmother had insulted her when sh-- they got married or who knows when. And she would never have anything to do with his family. And I was from his family. And blood is thicker than water. I heard that every day, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, like, not long after that, she told me, and you know how much money he spent? He took this man out to get an affidavit. I could have had a new fur coat. That I heard every day also. She was very nasty. She did not want me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I met somebody. I was very lonely. And I had nobody to talk to except them. Then in the, in the elevator, there was a young woman who heard me speak German. She talked to me. And she said, you know, I'm from Germany. And come and visit me. I live down in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to visit her. And I found out she was a nurse. She was, she was hired by these people to take care of their children or child-- I don't remember what it was. Her name was Betty. And she said, you know, you can get a job like that. Because right away, I told her all my problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the other things she-- my, my aunt did, I wrote right away. She made me open a letter and tell her, show her what I wrote to my mother. And if I got a letter, she opened it first and read it and then gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were getting correspondence from your parents at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your mother, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my mother, yeah. It took awhile. It took like two weeks to get a letter and come back. But my aunt opened-- she had-- she thought she had the right to open my letters before I read them. I was very upset about that. Because the first letter I wrote, and I wrote about the living arrangements. And I thought-- I just said it was strange. Because I'd never seen anything like that. And right away, I was making problems. I was making trouble between them. I had nothing to do-- I mean, when these two together, there was never a good word between them. They were always fighting, always fighting. And he had her down to a certain point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every morning before he went to the pool hall, he would give her $2 for her day's grocery. That's was her money. He never gave her any other money unless she really needed for laundry. And laundry went to the Chinese laundry. And otherw-- and you washed it, in a, in a, on a thing in the basement, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I made myself-- you know, at first, I immediately wanted to show her that I didn't want to be-- I did all her cleaning. And she found out I knew all the Hungarian cooking that she had forgotten. And I made all these things. She liked that. And she liked the fact that I cleaned for her and did all the things and sometimes went down the store for her when she forgot something. And it was difficult in the beginning. Because I had learned a little bit of English in the last couple of months before I went from some British woman. But in Brooklyn, to me, they didn't speak English. That accent was so horrible. I said, that's not English. Yes, that's English. And then those two spoke with very heavy accents still.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get a-- did you find a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I asked Betty-- that was my new friend. She said, you know. She said, you don't have to live there like that. You know, I told her all my problems. She says, you can get a job like I have. And you can be independent. So I said, well, how do I find a job? She said, I'll ask my-- the woman I work for. And sure enough, she knew of a woman on the same-- in the same street who was looking for somebody. And I went over there didn't tell my aunt. And I went over there. And the interview was a riot. She spoke Yiddish, and I spoke German. And in between, so we sort of understood each other. Because I didn't know how to speak Yiddish. I spoke German. To me-- But I understand her. And she understood me a little bit. And we sort of got along. And mainly, she wanted somebody to take care of the baby. She had just of couple of months old baby, I think three or four months. And I had to sleep in the baby's room and feed it at night and take it out. I had to wear a white uniform. I agreed to everything. I was to get $35 a month. I was going to get Thursday afternoons off. And once a month, I got a Sunday afternoon off. And I agreed to everything, of course. When I went back and tell my aunt, she was very unhappy about it. That surprised me. And so was he. They didn't want me to take the job. I said, look, I have to earn some money. Because that money goes to the tickets for my parents. And I have to send some money. Maybe they need some money. I have to send some money. I have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So therefore, I just went, and I took the job. They didn't like the idea. She didn't like the idea she lost a free maid, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but before I went there, she said I have to stay for two weeks. I can't take the job for two weeks. Because every two-- every year, she goes to the country for two weeks. Her sons paid for her. She had three sons. Her sons were something else. One of them tried to pick, you know-- put his hands on me. And I got very angry-- you know, tried to feel me up and down. And I said, stop that. And don't you ever touch me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, you said you had correspondence with your parents. When did the correspondence stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately. I stopped-- it didn't stop. It stopped when the war started. Because then there was no more mail, because the boats didn't take mail anymore. You know, it was a war going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the newspaper-- see from this job-- I mean, the job lasted for about six months, I think. Because I couldn't speak English. And he got a lot of-- I think he was either a lawyer or an accountant. He got a lot of phone calls. I had to take messages. I had a hard time understanding people as it was. On the phone, I couldn't understand anybody. And I loused up some messages. And they fired me. So I was very unhappy. And my aunt, of course, immediately said, well, I knew you weren't going to keep a job. You're not going to keep a job. She was always very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nasty to me. She didn't want to go out with me, because I looked like a refugee. And my biggest shock was the anti-Semitism that I found in the '30s in New York. It was horrible. I thought I was back in Vienna. Nobody wanted the refugees to come here. Nobody believed that the Jews went through-- oh, they must have done something. Nobody believed that anybody could go to a concentration camp unless they did something. They must have done something. You don't just lock up people. They didn't understand. This was before anybody knew anything. And nobody believed. And even people that came out from the concentration camps that came here and told of what went on, they weren't believed. They were not believed. And then there was this horrible Catholic priest who came on the radio. His name was Father Coughlin-- I don't know whether you read about him-- who went into tirades over the radio about the Jews and how wonderful Hitler was and what we should do what-- to the Jews what he's doing to the Jews. It was just horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went from this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back. And, and I said, Betty, how do I get? She says, don't worry. Look in the paper. They always advertise. So I got another job. I took a job with a family that had two children that were not babies. I thought it would be easier. How, how, how, silly I was. They ran me ragged. And not only this, she-- they rented a house in Peekskill. And I went with them for the summer for one month into Peekskill. The only nice thing about it was that on my day off, I used to hitchhike all over the place. It was really lovely. In those days, you could do that. Nobody thought anything of it. Everybody was hitchhiking in those days. It was perfectly safe. And so I went-- was with them for a while. And then somebody said, you know, you could get something. I wanted something else. Then I got a job. Then I decided, maybe this is too much for me. I was always running ragged with the kids. Maybe I should take a job as a housekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I found a job as a housekeeper. And I was there, like, one day. And I went the refrigerator to get a cold drink. And there was a lock on the refrigerator. So I went to the woman who had claimed when she hired me that she did so much for the Jewish refugees. I said, why is there a lock on it? She says she doesn't believe in eating in between. I said I wanted a cold drink. I didn't want to eat. And besides, I don't like to be told when I should eat and when I shouldn't eat. That is not part of our agreement. I said, and how many, and how many maids did you have that left? Because this one is about to leave. So I stayed another couple of days and left. OK, let's go on with your life. You were with your uncle and aunt for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long-- maybe three months, four months. I don't really know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this one job from, from my friend, Betty. And then I got jobs on my own from the newspapers. And then somebody said to me when-- that's when-- oh, Betty got tired of having this kind of job, too. She got a job for Bartlett's Candy. In those days, everything was done by hand. And she had to put the things in there. But she couldn't get me a job. And I thought it was terrific. She got $12 a week. That was a fortune. I got $35 a month, you know. And I saved practically everything. I found out that I could-- if I needed something, shoes, I could get for $2. Dress, I could get for $1 in Macy's, you know, when there was a sale. I barely bought anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once I bought a heavy coat or a jacket because I was cold. And I didn't have anything that, you know-- so my aunt, she says, that's what you're spending your money on? You have no right to spend your money on this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other thing I immediately told my uncle, I would like to meet my my-- the man that gave the affidavit. I want to thank him. I want to tell him that we need what we need. No, no, no, he said. I will take care of everything. And you-- he's a busy man. He doesn't want to see you. And I couldn't get him to take me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I got from this job, what did I do? Oh, yeah. She said, why don't you try and get another job? I got a job selling things from house to house, like the Fuller Brush man [? is ?] [? stacked ?] with stuff. Because I couldn't get a job-- she couldn't get me into the Bartlett's Candy thing. Now, did you get-- did you meet anybody at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, if you get-- no, she said, if you get a job like that, we'll get a room together. And we'll live very cheaply, which we did. A room, a furnished room, [? you would get in a family ?] was $3 a week. So we each paid $1.50. Then she found one for $2. And we each paid $1. And what we ate, we bought a little-- food was very cheap. We bought a little bread for six-- $0.06, $0.06, and a little cheese. And that's what we ate. Or we went to to the, the automat. You could get a sandwich for $0.10. The-- it's no longer available, the automat.

words.text: [? you would get in a family ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had, you had various jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I had a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me also about your correspondence, getting back to, to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have all-- I have a lot of correpond--

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you did receive letters during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yeah. No, not during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, not during the war. It stopped at the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943. At that point, I had already other jobs. This job, I didn't like very much. And then somebody said, why don't you become a waitress? You don't have to know much English. I mean, you take the order. And you bring it. And I was learning-- within one year, by the way, I was fluent in English. I learned English. At the first person that I worked for, I found out from Betty that you could go to school for free at night for, you know, refugees, like they do today. And they let me-- if they weren't going out, they were going out a lot-- they let me go in the evening. And I took books. I mean, the library was open. And I was able to find the library right away, always. And I used to take the books. And I sat with the books and the, and the, and the, and the, and the, book, the word book, you know, and newspaper. And I tried to read the newspaper, because that was very important to me. And when I was not reading, when I had my day off, I used to go to the movies. It was $0.10. I used to go. I'd go down to Times Square. It was $0.10. It cost a nickel to go down, a nickel to come back. The movie was $0.10. I would buy a hot dog for a nickel and maybe a cup of coffee for a nickel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your family again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all this went on. In the meantime, I asked my mother whether I should send the money. She said, no, don't send money. Save the money for the tickets and get the affidavits. Get the affidavits. Get the affidavits. That was all we were constantly talking about, when we would get the affidavits, when they would be able to come. In 1941, before, before the war started, in the beginning of '41, my father had been released from the concentration camp. They did that in those days to show that they weren't-- you know, it was before they decided to kill all the Jews. And they let some people go to show that it wasn't what they thought they were going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got a letter from my mother. That's-- I think I got this letter-- where he says, my poor, my poor daughter. You're all alone. I'm thinking of you all the time. He is sorry for me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I always wanted to send money. And she always said, no, save the money. Save the money. Do not send any money. We're doing all right. We're-- you know, the relatives are helping me. We're doing all right. Don't worry about it. I want you to save the money. So I did. And that was in 1941. And he was very anxious for-- to come. And so-- and I asked my uncle again to please, you know, do something. At that point, my uncle had told me, you know, I'm taking this man out. And I am buying him expensive liquor. And I take him out to dinner to fancy places. And I need some money. So I had to give him some-- I gave him money.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the man that was trying-- he was trying to get the affidavit?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle took money from me. Constantly, he kept on saying, I need some money to take him out. I need this. Of course, I couldn't say no. I didn't know that was he was lying. Then when-- finally, I got to the point where I didn't know what to do. So I looked up the man in the phone book. I knew his name, knew he lived in Brooklyn someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I called him up. And I introduced myself. And I said, I would like to come and see you. He said, of course. He gave me directions. I went there one evening by myself, all by myself. I didn't tell anybody. And I met him. And he turned out to be a very nice man. And he-- the first thing he said, how's your Uncle Joe?' I said, haven't you seen him? He said, no, I haven't seen him in months. So I found out he never did anything. So I explained to him why I came and thanked him for bringing me over and that I needed an affidavit for my family. He says, of course. No pres-- no, no problem. You'll have it within a week. Oh, you want me to stop? No. You'll have it within a week. I said, I will send it. Just give it to me. And I'll do all the paperwork. He says, I'll let you know. And, and he says, come in a week. We made an appointment. I came. And he had all of the stuff there. I went immediately. I sent it registered, I think, sent it all over. And of course then, I had another problem. How am I going to tell my uncle that I went behind his back? I went behind his back. And I figured, well maybe if he hasn't seen him, maybe he won't find out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, when I went to-- I still went to visit, visit them. Because they were my only family, I figured I shouldn't break up, you know, even though they were mad at me, mostly my aunt. She got friendlier with me than he did. He, he sort of kept his distance. Because he used to take me to the pool hall. And the guys would talk to me and make faces at me. And I was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We-- let's move on to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your future.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your family. So your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Never made it here to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. So I went. I went-- one day I went back. And he asked me for quite a consistent amount of money. He asked me for a lot of money. He says, I have to take him out. Otherwise, he's not going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's when I had to tell him. I said, you know, I already went there. Well, he was, he was so furious, I thought he was going to hit me. He just went into a total rage, in such a rage. You went behind my back? This is what you get when you-- this is the thanks I get for bringing you over here? You go behind my back. And you, you make appointments behind my back to people that you don't even know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he was-- and then he threatened me. He said, you know-- in those days, 21 was the age that you were an adult, not 18 like now. I was 18 or 19. He said, you know, I'm your guardian. I want you to know I can put you away someplace if you don't listen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got scared to death. And I went to my friend, Betty. And she said, don't be silly. You're earning your own money. You didn't do anything wrong. He can't put you anywhere. Don't worry about it. But he never spoke to me after that. OK. Let's talk about your future, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: From that, from that particular job, which I didn't like, I became a waitress. And I had-- sometimes I had, through an agency-- sometimes I had three different jobs-- one for breakfast, one for lunch, and one for dinner. These were all temp jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that point, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was-- that comes a little later. I told you, this is the longest part. This is a very long part. You don't want me to say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I became a waitress. And then I met somebody that said they were opening up a restaurant in Peekskill, in Peeksill-- not in Peekskill-- in the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mountains, the Jewish ghetto there in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Catskills.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Catskill, the Catskills, yes-- and he wanted somebody, not only as a waitress, but he had two children. And in the daytime, I should take care of his children. So I figured anything is better. In the meantime, Betty had a boyfriend who came. And they got married. And I was alone anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave up my room. And I went out to, to the Catskills, Ellenville. And I became a waitress. And I never forget what happened there. And all of the people he hired were all refugees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one man, tall, thin man with a white chef's hat and white chef's hat. And he was supposed to be the cook. And he was standing there in the kitchen, looking at some raw meat and some vegetables. So he looked at it with a German cookbook in his hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, have you ever cooked a meal before? He said, no. I said, why did you take the job? He said, I needed a job. I have to go to school to get a degree or whatever he was doing. And in the meantime, I have to eat

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I figured all I had to do-- somebody said all he had to do was, was turn frankfurters on a, on a grill, or hamburgers. He didn't know he had to cook for the family and everybody else in this. So I cooked a meal. And I taught him a little thing. There was one other waiter. They were all German. Everybody was German, so at least I had somebody to talk to. At-- except at this point, I spoke pretty fluent. I spoke better than my p-- my relatives did. I had a better accent than they had. And I spoke more fluent than they had, which they also noticed. And they didn't like that, especially my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anyway, I was there for a while. Then one night, I woke up in the middle of the night. And the guy is standing in front of me. And he said his wife had to go away. And am I not lonely? Wouldn't I have some-- like to have some company? I said, if you don't go away this minute, I'll scream. And he went out. So then I talked to this other man who was also there. And I said, what happened? He said, well, you can't stay here. I said, I know I can't stay here. Because he maybe is going to get worse. So he said, I'll, I'll cover for you. Can go look for-- there were lots of hotels there. So I took to-- made the rounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found a little family hotel where the people came during the, during the summer. And they had children. And I became a children's waitress. They, they ate separately in separate rooms and separate things.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK Rita, let's go forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there. Then when the summer ended, I went back. And I went back to, to waitressing. Then I finally had made arrange-- you know, though Betty, I met some other people. And they had some organizations there. And somebody always, you know, always gave out information of where you could find a job. Then somebody said, you know, there's a new, new something is coming here in Belgium. And by that time, he had taken over Belgium. Hitler had taken over Belgium and France-- that when I was in the Catskills, I remember we all cried when we saw a picture of the Nazis marching down the Arc de Triomphe. We all cried. And then he had taken over Belgium. And he said all the Belgian diamond-- the diamond business is coming over here. And there is a place down, down, down near Canal Street. It's called [? Dunehoffer's. ?] And they hire women now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down there. And I got a job for $12 a week. To me, that was a fortune. I'd never made $12 a week. I'd lived on-- oh yeah, before that, I also tried-- the Jewish agency gave a, a class for people that wanted to learn sewing and go into the garment district. I took this course. And they supported me, like, with $7 a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after that, they wouldn't give you a job. You learned how to put-- use the machine and how to put things together. You had to go around to the various unions. And I went around, made my way. And I couldn't get a job. This-- if you had a little experience in Europe, they got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then somebody once said, oh, yeah, you want a job? I tell you, come to a, come to a party tonight. And we'll get you settled. I told this to Betty. She says, don't go to any parties. You don't know what they do there. So that was finished. So when I came back, I said, you know, I come to your party after you get me a job. He says, oh, if you don't come to the party, there's no job. That was it. So that's when I got this job out in the Catskills.
